strings.string: FASTER Daily Scale
F.A.S.T.E.R.
Options
Options
Language
Home
Profile
Log out
English
Spanish
What is F.A.S.T.E.R.?

The F.A.S.T.E.R. scale helps you recognize where you are on your restoration journey. Each letter represents a stage of decline; the first (Restoration) is the ideal state. Review the pillars honestly to identify areas that need attention.
F — Forgetting Priorities

It is the beginning of spiritual decline. The person begins to neglect communion with God and eternal priorities. There is no open fall yet, but an internal shift: less prayer, less vigilance, more distraction and self-sufficiency.
A — Anxiety
An internal noise of worry and fear arises. Practical trust in God decreases and thoughts become disordered. Resentment, speculation, and emotional tension appear.
S — Speeding Up

It is the fleshly response to anxiety. Instead of stopping and depending on God, the person speeds up: overcommits, works compulsively, or fills their schedule to avoid facing what they feel. Doing replaces resting in God.
T — Ticked Off (Anger / Constant Irritation)
Accumulated frustration turns into anger. There are overreactions, pride, and defensiveness. The person becomes more critical, less patient, and more prone to conflict.
E — Exhaustion
After living in anxiety, speeding up, and anger, collapse comes. There is physical, emotional, and spiritual fatigue. Joy and clarity are lost. It is a state of extreme vulnerability.
R — Relapse

It is the return to previously abandoned sinful or addictive behaviors. The person gives in to desire, loses control, and experiences guilt and shame. It is the final result of the downward process if not interrupted earlier.

How to use the program: Go through the pillars in order. In each one, check the phrases that describe your situation, fill in “Most powerful behavior” and the questions. Use Back and Next, or End to go to the pillars summary. The 💪 button moves that phrase to “Most powerful behavior”. If you fill a pillar, all previous ones must have something too. View Summary shows the last pillar with data and its advice.
Start
View my records
Move to most powerful behavior
Most powerful behavior
How does it affect me? How do I feel in that moment?
How does it affect the important people in my life?
Why do I do it? What is the benefit for me?
Back
Next
Review My Pillars
Finish
Restoration

It is the state of walking in the light: living without hiding sin, practicing confession, obedience, gratitude, and communion with God and other believers. It reflects a life aligned with truth, with accountability, humility, and continual growth in sanctification.
Forgetting priorities

It is the beginning of spiritual cooling. The heart begins to shift from God toward temporal concerns. Secrets, distractions, and self-sufficiency appear. It is not yet open fall, but an internal deviation from first love.
Anxiety

It is a state where the heart loses rest in God’s sovereignty and begins to be dominated by fear, disordered thoughts, and intense emotions. It reflects a practical loss of trust in God’s character and providential control.
Speeding up
It is a fleshly response to anxiety: hyperactivity, self-sufficiency, and excessive effort to control outcomes. Doing replaces depending, and rest in God is lost.
Getting angry
It is when pride and frustration take control. Sinful anger replaces meekness, and the heart hardens. Being right is sought more than reflecting Christ’s character.
Exhaustion
It is the consequence of living in prolonged tension outside of rest in God. There is loss of joy and hope, and the person functions in survival mode, vulnerable to falling back into old patterns.
Relapse
It is the conscious return to previously abandoned patterns of sin. It involves giving in to desire, taking control independent of God, and experiencing renewed guilt and shame.
Pillars
Review which pillars have information. You can go to any of them to complete or edit.
You must fill this pillar
View Summary
Summary
You are at
Start Over

Advice:

Yes, delete all

Are you sure you want to delete all records? This action cannot be undone.
If you check at least one box, you must fill in all text fields: {0}.
You must complete at least one pillar.
Some pillars are incomplete. You must go back and fill: {0}.
Record saved.
Select at least one record to export.
F.A.S.T.E.R. — Exported records
Record —
Date:

Remain in the Word daily, pray consistently, keep active fellowship with other believers, and practice immediate confession when you fail. Perseverance in the means of grace (Scripture, prayer, church, and accountability) is what sustains restoration.

Recognize the drift and repent deliberately. Restore defined times of prayer and Scripture, seek accountability, and remove distractions that compete with your communion with God. Restoration begins when you put God first again.
Renew your mind with biblical truth. Replace speculative thoughts with clear promises from Scripture. Pray, casting your burdens on God with thanksgiving. Peace grows when truth rules the mind.

Practice intentional rest. Reduce unnecessary activity and set aside times of silence, prayer, and biblical reflection. Remember that faithfulness is measured not by activity but by dependent obedience.
Confess the pride that feeds anger. Practice deliberate humility and seek quick reconciliation. Meditate on the grace you have received, and let that mercy shape how you treat others.

Return to the essentials: adequate physical rest, sincere prayer, and fellowship with mature believers. Do not try to fix everything at once; prioritize restoring your relationship with God and trusting His sustaining grace.
Do not hide in shame. Confess immediately, repent genuinely, and remove sources of temptation. Cling to the gospel: there is real forgiveness and power to get up again in obedience.
This week I was honest with myself and others, with no significant secrets
I worked actively to resolve pending problems
I identified my fears and feelings and faced them
I kept my commitments to meetings, prayer, family, and friends
I practiced gratitude and vulnerability with others
I increased my connection and trust in God and significant people
I was open, honest, and made eye contact in important interactions
I have kept secrets that affect my spiritual life or relationships
I have spent less time or energy on prayer, meetings, or church activities
I have avoided support or accountability people
I have had superficial instead of deep conversations
I have changed important goals or priorities without clear reason
I have been obsessed with relationships or material matters
I have broken promises or commitments
I have neglected my family or close people
I have sought to control situations or other people
I have been distracted by entertainment, TV, computer, or excessive leisure
I have lied or exaggerated to improve my image with others
I have used sarcasm or passive-aggressive attitudes
I have been emotionally isolated from others
I have procrastinated on important matters
I have acted overconfidently, minimizing risks
I have felt constant boredom that leads me to distract myself
I have hidden money or financial information
I have denied real problems or fled important conversations
I have worried constantly or felt undefined fear
I have used harsh words or been irritable
I have felt resentment toward someone
I have judged the intentions or motives of others
I have tried to set goals or lists I cannot fulfill
I have "read the mind" of others without evidence
I have fantasized or tried to rescue others in a codependent way
I have had concentration or sleep problems
I have sought or created unnecessary drama
I have used over-the-counter medications (for pain, sleep, or weight control)
I have flirted inappropriately
I have repeated old negative thoughts about myself or my past
I have acted from perfectionism or fear of making mistakes
I have participated in gossip or harmful conversations about others
I have kept myself super busy to avoid my emotions
I have overworked or felt like a workaholic
I have avoided relaxing or slowing down
I have felt impulsivity or difficulty turning off my thoughts
I have skipped meals or had binge eating
I have overspent money
I have had difficulty identifying my feelings or needs
I have repeated negative thoughts constantly
I have been irritable or had drastic mood swings
I have consumed too much caffeine or stimulants
I have over-exercised
I have had difficulty being alone or with others
I have made excuses to "do it all"
I have felt constant physical nervousness
I have had real difficulty listening to others attentively
I have procrastinated, causing problems at work, with money, or in relationships
I have increased sarcasm or hurtful comments
I have thought in "all or nothing" terms
I have felt alone or misunderstood
I have overreacted or reacted with rage
I have had constant resentments
I have pushed others away or increased my isolation
I have blamed others or argued
I have had irrational thinking or not accepted criticism
I have been defensive or needed to be right
I have had physical problems from stress (headache, digestion)
I have had obsessive thoughts or difficulty forgiving
I have felt superior or intimidated others
I have noticed people avoiding me because of my attitude or reactions
I have felt depressed, overwhelmed, or hopeless
I have had panic attacks or confusion
I have slept too much or too little
I have felt I cannot cope with the situation
I have cried for no apparent reason
I have forgotten important things
I have had negative or pessimistic thoughts about myself
I have felt helpless, tired, or numb
I have wanted to run away or escape
I have had cravings for old destructive behaviors
I have sought unhealthy people or places
I have isolated myself a lot
Others have been angry with me because of my behavior
I have mistreated or neglected myself
I have had suicidal thoughts or intense hopelessness
I have missed important commitments or work
I have been functioning in "survival mode", doing only the minimum to get through the day
I have given in and lost control over my addictive behaviors
I have lied to myself or others about my situation
I have felt unable to handle life without resorting to past behaviors
I have experienced intense guilt, shame, or condemnation
I have felt abandoned or alone
I have returned to behaviors I promised not to return to
I have decided to run my life on my own terms instead of trusting God
